does: This paper systematically evaluates jailbreak detection methods for large language models by sampling multiple outputs from different model variants. The authors test both a lexical TF-IDF detector and a generation inconsistency detector against the JailbreakBench dataset, examining how detection performance improves as the number of sampled generations increases. Key findings show that single-generation evaluation substantially underestimates vulnerability, with the largest detection gains occurring when increasing from one to several outputs, while further increases show diminishing returns. The work quantifies this trade-off between sampling budget and detection reliability across models with varying alignment strengths.
connects: This work responds to the growing challenge of jailbreak detection in increasingly capable language models, building on prior research distinguishing between harmful intent and model vulnerability. It extends earlier detection approaches like lexical analysis and consistency-based methods to realistic multi-sampling scenarios. The paper connects to broader work on model alignment evaluation and adversarial robustness testing, incorporating lessons from red-teaming methodologies that recognize misalignment may manifest inconsistently across generations. By using the JailbreakBench dataset, it aligns with community efforts to standardize jailbreak evaluation and leverages existing frameworks for assessing alignment failures.
enables: This empirical foundation supports more reliable jailbreak detection protocols and safer deployment practices for production systems. The quantified sampling-performance trade-off enables practical guidance on auditing budgets needed to achieve acceptable false-negative rates. Future work can build on these insights by developing adaptive sampling strategies that allocate generation budget intelligently based on initial signals, or by designing detectors that more effectively leverage cross-generational consistency patterns. The cross-model generalization findings suggest potential for transfer learning in detection, while category-level analysis opens pathways toward behavior-specific detection methods that account for heterogeneous jailbreak mechanisms.
